title: Le Vieux Temps
content:
Venez ici, mon cher ami, an' sit down by meï¿½so
An' I will tole you story of old tam long agoï¿½
W'en ev'ryt'ing is happyï¿½w'en all de bird is sing
An' me!ï¿½I'm young an' strong lak moose an' not afraid no t'ing.
I close my eye jus' so, an' see de place w'ere I am bornï¿½
I close my ear an' lissen to musique of de horn,
Dat 's horn ma dear ole moder blowï¿½an only t'ing she play
Is "viens donc vite Napolï¿½onï¿½'peche toi pour votre souper."ï¿½
An' w'en he 's hear dat nice musiqueï¿½ma leetle dog "Carleau"
Is place hees tail upon hees backï¿½an' den he 's let heem goï¿½
He 's jomp on fenceï¿½he 's swimmin' crikï¿½he 's ronne two forty gait,
He say "dat 's somet'ing good for eatï¿½Carleau mus' not be late."
O dem was pleasure day for sure, dem day of long ago
W'en I was play wit' all de boy, an' all de girl also;
An' many tam w'en I 'm alone an' t'ink of day gone by
An' pull latire an' spark de girl, I cry upon my eye.
Ma fader an' ma moder too, got nice, nice familee,
Dat 's ten garï¿½on an' t'orteen girl, was mak' it twenty t'ree
But fonny t'ing de Gouvernement don't geev de firs' prize den
Lak w'at dey say dey geev it now, for only wan douzaine.
De English peep dat only got wan familee small size
Mus' be feel glad dat tam dere is no honder acre prize
For fader of twelve chil'renï¿½dey know dat mus' be so,
De Canayens would boss Kebeckï¿½mebbe Ontario.
But dat is not de story dat I was gone tole you
About de fun we use to have w'en we leev a chez nous
We 're never lonesome on dat house, for many cavalier
Come at our place mos' every nightï¿½especially Sun-day.
But tam I 'member bes' is w'en I 'm twenty wan yearï¿½meï¿½
An' so for mak' some pleasementï¿½we geev wan large soirï¿½e
De whole paroisse she be inviteï¿½de Curï¿½ he 's come tooï¿½
Wit plaintee peep from 'noder placeï¿½dat 's more I can tole you.
De night she 's cole an' freeze also, chemin she 's fill wit snow
An' on de chimley lak phantome, de win' is mak' it blowï¿½
But boy an' girl come all de sam an' pass on grande parloir
For warm itself on beeg box stove, was mak' on Trois Riviï¿½resï¿½
An' w'en Bonhomme Latour commence for tune up hees fidelle
It mak' us all feel very gladï¿½l'enfant! he play so well,
Musique suppose to be firs' class, I offen hear, for sure
But mos' bes' man, beat all de res', is ole Bateese Latourï¿½
An' w'en Bateese play Irish jeeg, he 's learn on Mattawa
Dat tam he 's head boss cook Shainteeï¿½den leetle Joe Leblanc
Tak' hole de beeg Marie Juneau an' dance upon de floor
Till Marie say "Excuse to me, I cannot dance no more."ï¿½
An' den de Curï¿½ 's mak' de speechï¿½ole Curï¿½ Ladouceur!
He say de girl was spark de boy too much on some cornerreï¿½
An' so he 's tole Bateese play up ole fashion reel a quatre
An' every body she mus' dance, dey can't get off on dat.
Away she goï¿½hooraw! hooraw! plus fort Bateese, mon vieux
Camille Bisson, please watch your girlï¿½dat 's bes' t'ing you can do.
Pass on de right an' tak' your place Mamzelle Des Trois Maisons
You 're s'pose for dance on Paul Laberge, not Telesphore Gagnon.
Mon oncle Al-fred, he spik lak' datï¿½'cos he is boss de floor,
An' so we do our possibill an' den commence encore.
Dem crowd of boy an' girl I'm sure keep up until nex' day
If ole Bateese don't stop heseff, he come so fatiguï¿½.
An' affer dat, we eat some t'ing, tak' leetle drink also
An' de Curï¿½, he 's tole story of many year agoï¿½
W'en Iroquois sauvage she 's keel de Canayens an' steal deir hair,
An' say dat 's only for Bon Dieu, we don't be hereï¿½he don't be dere.
But dat was mak' de girl feel scareï¿½so all de cavalier
Was ax hees girl go home right off, an' place her on de sleigh,
An' w'en dey start, de Curï¿½ say, "Bonsoir et bon voyage
Menagez-vousï¿½tak' care for youï¿½prenez-garde pour les sauvages."
An' den I go meseff also, an' tak' ma belle Elmireï¿½
She 's nicer girl on whole Comtï¿½, an' jus' got eighteen yearï¿½
Black hairï¿½black eye, an' chick rosï¿½e dat 's lak wan fameuse on de fall
But don't spik muchï¿½not of dat kin', I can't say she love me at all.
Ma girlï¿½she's fader beeg farmeurï¿½leev 'noder side St. Flore
Got five-six honder acreï¿½mebbe a leetle moreï¿½
Nice sugar bushï¿½une belle maisonï¿½de bes' I never seeï¿½
So w'en I go for spark Elmire, I don't be mak' de foolish meï¿½
Elmire!ï¿½she 's pass t'ree year on schoolï¿½Ste. Anne de la Perade
An' w'en she 's tak' de firs' class prize, dat 's mak' de ole man glad;
He say "Ba goshï¿½ma girl can washï¿½can keep de kitchen clean
Den change her dressï¿½mak' politesse before God save de Queen."
Dey 's many way for spark de girl, an' you know dat of course,
Some way dey might be better way, an' some dey might be worse
But I lak' sit some cole night wit' my girl on ole burleau
 Wit' lot of hay keep our foot warmï¿½an' plaintee buffaloï¿½
Dat 's geev good chances get acquaintï¿½an' if burleau upset
An' t'row you out upon de snowï¿½dat 's better chances yetï¿½
An' if you help de girl go home, if horse he ronne away
De girl she 's not much use at allï¿½don't geev you nice baiser!
Dat 's very well for fun ma frien', but w'en you spark for keep
She 's not sam t'ing an' mak' you feel so scare lak' leetle sheep
Some tam you get de feverï¿½some tam you 're lak snowball
An' all de tam you ack lak' fouï¿½can't spik no t'ing at all.
Wall! dat 's de way I feel meseff, wit Elmire on burleau,
Jus' lak' small dog try ketch hees tailï¿½roun' roun' ma head she go
But bimeby I come more braveï¿½an' tak' Elmire she's han'
"Laisee-moi tranquille" Elmire she say "You mus' be crazy man."
"Yassï¿½yass I say " mebbe you t'ink I 'm wan beeg loup garou,
Dat 's forty t'ousand 'noder girl, I lef' dem all for you,
I s'pose you know Polique Gauthier your frien' on St. Cesaire
I ax her marry me nex' wickï¿½she tak' meï¿½I don't care."
Ba gosh; Elmire she don't lak datï¿½it mak' her feel so madï¿½
She commence cry, say "'Poleon you treat me very badï¿½
I don't lak see you t'row you'seff upon Polique Gauthier,
So if you say you love me sureï¿½we mak' de marieï¿½"ï¿½
Oh it was fine tam affer datï¿½Castor I t'ink he know,
We 're not too busy for get homeï¿½he go so nice an' slow,
He 's only upset t'reeï¿½four tamï¿½an' jus' about daylight
We pass upon de ole man's placeï¿½an' every t'ing 's all right.
Wall! we leev happy on de farm for nearly fifty year,
Till wan day on de summer tamï¿½she dieï¿½ma belle Elmire
I feel so lonesome lef' behin'ï¿½I tink 't was bes' mebbeï¿½
Dat w'en le Bon Dieu tak' ma fammeï¿½he should not forget me.
But dat is hees biz-nesse ma frien'ï¿½I know dat 's all right dere
I 'll wait till he call "'Poleon" den I will be prepareï¿½
An' w'en he fin' me ready, for mak' de longue voyage
He guide me t'roo de wood hesef upon ma las' portage.
meaning:
About: William Henry Drummond (April 13, 1854 ï¿½ April 6, 1907) was an Irish-born Canadian poet.

He was elected a fellow of the Royal Society of Literature of the United Kingdom in 1898 and a fellow of the Royal Society of Canada in 1899.
